3	description: Per-file bidirectional verifier. Reads one .lean file and its blueprint chapter; reports (a) whether the Lean file follows the blueprint — fake/placeholder statements, signature mismatches with \lean{...} hints, proof divergence, "temporary" defs — AND (b) whether the blueprint chapter is detailed enough to have guided a faithful formalization of this file.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended in the review phase. Dispatch one checker
11	    per `.lean` file that received prover work this iteration. Each
12	    dispatch is independent and parallel-able.
13	
14	    **You may skip me this iter when NO `.lean` file received prover
15	    work** (e.g. the prover phase was skipped, or every prover
16	    INCOMPLETE'd without committing edits). Record under `## Subagent
17	    skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
18	    ``- lean-vs-blueprint-checker: no .lean files modified this iter
19	    (no prover edits to verify)``.
20	
21	    Do NOT skip a per-file dispatch when the prover DID commit edits
22	    to that file — skipping a prover-touched file is the review-phase
23	    failure this affordance exists to surface, not to enable. The
24	    "highly recommended" framing applies to the whole-iter decision,
25	    not to picking-and-choosing within an iter that has multiple
26	    prover-touched files.
27	  - Also dispatch on any file a code-audit subagent in your catalog
28	    flagged as suspicious, even if it received no prover work this
29	    iter.

31	    chapter — no global context, no strategy snapshot. My value is
32	    the narrow file-vs-chapter view.
33	  - I report bidirectionally: Lean → blueprint AND blueprint → Lean.
34	    The blueprint can be the failure (too thin to guide
35	    formalization, missing the level of detail the Lean code clearly
36	    needed), not only the Lean. If I flag a chapter as inadequate,

38	    subagent to address it.

40	    the affected file/chapter until addressed. Soft enforcement: the
41	    plan agent reads my report and follows the gate rules in plan.md.
42	---
43	
44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Check Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Iteration
128	<NNN>
129	
130	## Files audited
131	- Lean: <path>
132	- Blueprint: <path>
133	
134	## Per-declaration
135	
136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.
208

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Under Claude Code, skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Under Claude Code, rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — under Claude Code this is installed as the `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source), providing slash commands `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc. Under other harnesses the slash-command surface is absent; the underlying skill scripts are still on disk under `.claude/skills/` and can be run directly from the shell.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts — that path is the same whatever harness you run under. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) surface as `mcp__*` tools: under Claude Code they are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json`; under other harnesses the loop wires the same servers in at launch.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned agent reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — wait by staying inside it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and returns only once the child finishes and its report is on disk). A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read (edits via `archon discuss`) |
57	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
58	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
60	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
61	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
62	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
65	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
66	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
67	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
68	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
70	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
71	
72	### Subagent permissions
73	
74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
75	
76	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
77	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
78	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
79	
80	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
81	
82	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.
83	
84	## Protected Declarations
85	
86	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
87	
88	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
89	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
90	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
91	
92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
104	
105	## User Interaction
106	
107	The user provides hints in two places:
108	
109	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
117	
118	## Agent Roles
119	
120	### Plan Agent
121	
122	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
123	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
124	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
125	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
126	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
127	- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` — the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.
128	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
129	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
130	
131	### Prover Agent
132	
133	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
134	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
135	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
136	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
137	- A prover lane ends by writing its task result and exiting normally. **Do not kill Lean, Lake, shell, or sibling agent processes from inside the prover lane.** Do not run `pkill`, `killall`, regex-based process cleanup, or background-verification cleanup. If a verification command is still running, wait for its result or report that it is still running; process lifetime is owned by the Archon runner/orchestrator.
138	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
139	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
140	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
141	
142	### Subagents
143	
144	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
145	
146	### Review Agent
147	
148	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
149	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
150	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
151	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
152	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
153	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
154	
155	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
156	
157	These run automatically between phases:
158	
159	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
160	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
161	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

154	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
155	
156	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
157	
158	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
159	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
160	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
161	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
162	
163	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. The body lifts
164	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
165	the small Zariski site of `X` (fully defined, no `sorry`). -/
166	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
167	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
168	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
169	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
170	
171	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
172	
173	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
174	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
175	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
176	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
177	
178	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. The body inherits
179	the morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
180	sheafification (fully defined, no `sorry`). -/
181	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
182	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
183	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
184	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
185	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
186	
187	/-- **`⊗`-invertibility of an `𝒪_X`-module.** (Blueprint
188	`def:scheme_modules_isinvertible`.) `M : X.Modules` is `⊗`-invertible when it
189	admits a tensor inverse: an object `N` with `M ⊗_X N ≅ 𝒪_X`, where
190	`𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the designated unit. This is the
191	scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's `Module.Invertible`; the predicate carries its
192	inverse witness existentially, so the dual needed by the relative Picard group
193	law is definitional. -/
194	def IsInvertible {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
195	  ∃ N : X.Modules, Nonempty (tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
196

198

9	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
10	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
11	
12	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
13	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
14	\texttt{rel\_pic\_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
15	\[
16	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
17	  \qquad
18	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
19	\]
20	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
21	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
22	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
23	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
24	
25	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
26	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
27	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
28	% groups)
29	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
30	% SOURCE QUOTE:
31	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
32	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
33	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
34	% abelian groups defined by the formula
35	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
36	% \end{dfn}
37	%  [\dots]
38	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
39	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
40	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
41	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
42	% topologies  by
43	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
44	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
45	%  \end{dfn}"
46	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
47	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
48	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
49	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
50	homomorphisms.}
51	
52	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
53	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
54	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
55	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
56	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
57	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
58	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
59	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
60	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
61	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
62	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
63	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
64	structure. This is the content of
65	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
66	
67	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
68	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
69	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
70	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
71	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
72	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
73	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
74	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
75	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
76	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
77	\begin{enumerate}
78	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
79	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
80	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
81	    isomorphism classes;
82	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
83	    for \(\otimes\);
84	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
85	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
86	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
87	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
88	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
89	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
90	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
91	\end{enumerate}
92	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
93	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
94	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
95	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
96	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
97	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
98	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
99	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
100	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
101	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
102	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
103	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
104	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
105	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
106	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
107	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
108	supplied.
109	
110	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
111	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
112	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
113	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
114	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
115	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
116	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
117	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
118	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
119	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
120	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
121	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
122	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
123	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
124	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
125	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
126	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
127	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
128	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
129	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
130	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
131	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
132	avoids. The
133	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
134	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
135	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
136	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
137	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
138	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
139	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
140	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
141	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
142	
143	\section{Mathlib API survey}
144	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
145	
146	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
147	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
148	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
149	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
150	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
151	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
152	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
153	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
154	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
155	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
156	source.

163	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor

366	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (η.app MN)) := by
367	    rw [hη, PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf_map_sheafificationAdjunction_unit_app]
368	    exact CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.W_toSheafify _ _
369	  have hηNP : (Opens.grothendieckTopology X).W
370	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (η.app NP)) := by
371	    rw [hη, PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf_map_sheafificationAdjunction_unit_app]
372	    exact CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.W_toSheafify _ _
373	  have hW1 : (Opens.grothendieckTopology X).W
374	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (η.app MN ▷ P.val)) :=
375	    PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_W (R := X.presheaf) P.val (η.app MN) hηMN
376	  have hW3 : (Opens.grothendieckTopology X).W
377	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (M.val ◁ η.app NP)) :=
378	    PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_W (R := X.presheaf) M.val (η.app NP) hηNP
379	  -- Steps 1 and 3: the sheafification functor inverts the whiskered units.
380	  have hi1 : IsIso (a.map (η.app MN ▷ P.val)) :=
381	    PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val) _ hW1
382	  have hi3 : IsIso (a.map (M.val ◁ η.app NP)) :=
383	    PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val) _ hW3
384	  -- Step 2: the presheaf-of-modules associator, transported under `a`.
385	  have e2 := a.mapIso
386	    ((PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct (R := X.presheaf)).associator M.val N.val P.val)
387	  exact (@asIso _ _ _ _ _ hi1).symm ≪≫ e2 ≪≫ (@asIso _ _ _ _ _ hi3)
388	
389	/-- **Refining a trivialisation to a smaller open.** If `M` is trivialised on an
390	open `U` (`M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U`), it is trivialised on every open `W ≤ U`.
391	
392	The chart-chase is identical in spirit to `LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`:
393	factor `W.ι = (X.homOfLE hWU) ≫ U.ι`, transport through `restrictFunctorCongr`
394	and `restrictFunctorComp` to identify `M.restrict W.ι` with
395	`(M.restrict U.ι).restrict (X.homOfLE hWU)`, restrict the given trivialisation
396	`e` along that open immersion, and identify the restricted unit with the unit
397	via `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` + `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` (an isomorphism
398	because the open immersion's `Opens.map` is `Final`). -/
399	noncomputable def restrictIsoUnitOfLE {X : Scheme.{u}} {M : X.Modules} {U W : X.Opens}
400	    (hWU : W ≤ U)
401	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
402	    M.restrict W.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (W : Scheme).ringCatSheaf := by
403	  have hWU' : W ≤ (𝟙 X) ⁻¹ᵁ U := hWU
404	  set j : (W : Scheme) ⟶ (U : Scheme) := Scheme.Hom.resLE (𝟙 X) U W hWU' with hj
405	  have hjι : j ≫ U.ι = W.ι := by rw [hj, Scheme.Hom.resLE_comp_ι, Category.comp_id]
406	  haveI : (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map j.base).Final :=
407	    CategoryTheory.final_of_representablyFlat _
408	  -- M.restrict W.ι ≅ (pullback W.ι).obj M
409	  refine (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback W.ι).app M ≪≫ ?_
410	  -- ≅ (pullback (j ≫ U.ι)).obj M
411	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr hjι.symm).app M ≪≫ ?_
412	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj ((pullback U.ι).obj M)
413	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp j U.ι).symm.app M ≪≫ ?_
414	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj (M.restrict U.ι)
415	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso
416	    ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback U.ι).symm.app M) ≪≫ ?_
417	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj 𝒪_U
418	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso e ≪≫ ?_
419	  -- ≅ 𝒪_W
420	  haveI hI : IsIso (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit j.toRingCatSheafHom) := inferInstance
421	  exact @asIso _ _ _ _ _ hI
422	
423	/-- **Substrate tensor commutes with restriction along an open immersion.**
424	
425	For an open immersion `f : Y ⟶ X` and `M N : X.Modules`,
426	`(tensorObj M N).restrict f ≅ tensorObj (M.restrict f) (N.restrict f)`.
427	
428	This is the single substrate linchpin of `A.1.c.SubT` — **CLOSED, axiom-clean**
429	(iter-217). It says the substrate `⊗` (sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules
430	tensor) commutes with the restriction functor along an open immersion. The proof
431	is the blueprint's four-step composite:
432	  Step 1 (`restrictFunctorIsoPullback`): reduce `restrict` to the abstract pullback.
433	  Step 2 (`SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback`): move the pullback inside the
434	    sheafification (sheafification commutes with pullback).
435	  Step 3: strip the outer sheafification (`.mapIso`), descending to the presheaf goal
436	    `(pullback φ).obj (M.val ⊗ₚ N.val) ≅ (M.restrict f).val ⊗ₚ (N.restrict f).val`.
437	  Step 4: close that presheaf goal by **H1 ∘ H2**:
438	    • H1 (the sole Mathlib-ABSENT ingredient, BUILT this iter): the presheaf-level iso
439	      `pushforward β ≅ pullback φ`, obtained from the de-sheafified presheaf
440	      `PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj` (adjunction along the open-immersion
441	      pair `f.opensFunctor ⊣ Opens.map f.base`) against the existing
442	      `pullbackPushforwardAdjunction` via `Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq`. Here `β` is the
443	      `restrictFunctor` structure map, so `(M.restrict f).val = (pushforward β).obj M.val`
444	      definitionally.
445	    • H2 (strong-monoidal tensorator): `pushforward β = pushforward₀ ⋙ restrictScalars β`
446	      with `β` sectionwise the open-immersion ring ISO `f.appIso`, so `restrictScalars β`
447	      is STRONG monoidal (`restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective`, resting on the closed
448	      `restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv` / `restrictScalars_isIso_{μ,ε}`); the composite
449	      `μIso` is the tensorator.
450	The superseded `Localization.Monoidal` / `J.W.IsMonoidal` route is NOT used. -/
451	noncomputable def tensorObj_restrict_iso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X)
452	    [IsOpenImmersion f] (M N : X.Modules) :
453	    (tensorObj M N).restrict f ≅ tensorObj (M.restrict f) (N.restrict f) := by
454	  -- Step 1. Reduce `restrict` to `pullback` along the open immersion `f`
455	  -- (`restrictFunctorIsoPullback`, Mathlib).
456	  refine (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback f).app (tensorObj M N) ≪≫ ?_
457	  -- Step 2. **Sheafification commutes with pullback.** `tensorObj M N` is, by
458	  -- definition, `sheafification.obj (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj
459	  -- M.val N.val)`, so the genuine Mathlib lemma
460	  -- `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback`
461	  -- (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PullbackContinuous`,
462	  -- `sheafification ⋙ pullback φ ≅ PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom ⋙
463	  -- sheafification`) moves the pullback *inside* the sheafification. This
464	  -- discharges half (ii) of the original obstruction (sheafification commuting
465	  -- with pullback). After it the goal is the purely **presheaf-level** residual
466	  -- `sheafify ((PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ N.val))
467	  --    ≅ (M.restrict f).tensorObj (N.restrict f)`.
468	  refine (SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback f.toRingCatSheafHom).app
469	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj M.val N.val) ≪≫ ?_
470	  -- Step 3. **Strip the outer sheafification.** Both sides are
471	  -- `PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.obj)` applied to a
472	  -- presheaf-of-modules: the LHS to `(pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ₚ N.val)`, and
473	  -- the RHS `(M.restrict f).tensorObj (N.restrict f)` *by definition* to
474	  -- `(M.restrict f).val ⊗ₚ (N.restrict f).val`. So it suffices to give the
475	  -- comparison at the PRESHEAF level and sheafify it. This is a genuine reduction
476	  -- step (verified: the goal below has no sheafification).
477	  refine (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.obj)).mapIso ?_
478	  -- Step 4 (RESIDUAL CLOSURE — iter-217 H1 build). The remaining presheaf goal is
479	  --   `(PresheafOfModules.pullback φ).obj (M.val ⊗ₚ N.val)
480	  --      ≅ (M.restrict f).val ⊗ₚ (N.restrict f).val`
481	  -- where `φ = (Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom f).hom` and `⊗ₚ =
482	  -- PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`. We close it via:
483	  --  (H1, the linchpin) the presheaf-level iso `pushforward β ≅ pullback φ`, built from
484	  --      the presheaf `pushforwardPushforwardAdj` (above) against the existing
485	  --      `pullbackPushforwardAdjunction` via `leftAdjointUniq`. Here `β` is the
486	  --      open-immersion structure map of `restrictFunctor f`, so
487	  --      `(M.restrict f).val = (pushforward β).obj M.val` definitionally.
488	  --  (H2) the strong-monoidal comparison `(pushforward β).obj (A ⊗ₚ B) ≅
489	  --      (pushforward β).obj A ⊗ₚ (pushforward β).obj B`.
490	  -- `φR` (the scheme structure map) and `β` (the restrictFunctor structure map) are kept as
491	  -- `let`-bindings (zeta-transparent) so the unit/counit triangle goals below reduce; the
492	  -- open-immersion adjunction is INLINED for the same reason (a `have` would make `adj.unit`
493	  -- opaque and block the `congr` defeq, exactly as in Mathlib's sheaf-level `restrictAdjunction`).
494	  let φR := (Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom f).hom
495	  -- The restrictFunctor structure map `β` (so `(M.restrict f).val = (pushforward β).obj M.val`).
496	  let α : Y.presheaf ⟶ f.opensFunctor.op ⋙ X.presheaf :=
497	    { app := fun U => (f.appIso U.unop).inv }
498	  let β : Y.ringCatSheaf.obj ⟶ f.opensFunctor.op ⋙ X.ringCatSheaf.obj :=
499	    Functor.whiskerRight α (forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)

245	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
246	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a

250	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
251	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
252	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
253	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
254	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
255	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
256	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
257	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
258	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
259	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
260	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
261	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
262	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
263	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
264	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
265	the \emph{full} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or a coherent monoidal category.
266	The \emph{full monoidal-category} route~(e) is therefore off the critical path. The
267	associator does, however, consume a \emph{single} left-whisker existence lemma
268	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
269	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
270	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.
271	
272	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
273	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
274	is already present in Mathlib: the file
275	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
276	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
277	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
278	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
279	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
280	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
281	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
282	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
283	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
284	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
285	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
286	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
287	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
288	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
289	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
290	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
291	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
292	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
293	
294	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
295	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
296	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
297	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
298	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
299	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
300	independently of any upstream timing.

302	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
303	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
304	
305	\begin{definition}
306	
307	\leanok
308	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
309	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
311	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
312	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
313	  \[
314	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
315	  \]
316	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
317	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
318	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
320	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
321	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
322	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
323	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
324	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
325	  canonical compatibility
326	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
327	     \;\cong\;
328	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
329	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
330	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
331	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
332	
333	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
334	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
335	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
336	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
337	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
338	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
339	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
380	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
381	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
382	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
383	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
384	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
385	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
386	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
387	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
388	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
389	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:whisker_of_W}
396	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
397	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
398	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
399	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
400	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
401	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
402	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
403	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
404	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
405	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
406	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
407	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
408	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
409	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
410	
411	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
412	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
413	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
414	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
415	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
416	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
417	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
418	  fixed-base presheaves in
419	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
420	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
421	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \texttt{flat\_whisker\_localizer} (which is
422	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
423	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
424	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
425	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
426	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
427	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
428	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
429	
430	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
431	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
432	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
433	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
434	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
435	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
436	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
437	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
438	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
439	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
440	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
441	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
442	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
443	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
444	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
445	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
446	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
447	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
448	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
449	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
450	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
451	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
452	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
453	\end{remark}
454	
455	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
456	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
457	
458	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
459	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
460	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
461	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
462	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
463	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
464	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
465	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
466	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
468	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
469	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
470	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
471	\texttt{jw\_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
472	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
473	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
474	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
475	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
476	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
477	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
478	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
479	to the off-path route~(e).

554	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
555	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}

557	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
558	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
559	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
560	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
561	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
562	  morphism
563	  \[
564	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
565	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
566	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
567	  \]
568	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
569	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
570	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
571	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
572	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
573	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.

575	
576	\begin{proof}

579	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely

581	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
582	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
583	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an

585	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image

587	  isomorphism}

589	  \Gamma(U, V)\)

592	  therefore extension of scalars along

713	
714	\leanok
715	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
716	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
717	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
718	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
719	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
720	  product: the canonical map
721	  \[
722	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
723	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
724	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
725	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
726	  \]
727	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
728	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
729	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
730	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
731	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
732	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
733	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
734	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
735	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
736	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
737	\end{lemma}

739	\begin{lemma}
740	
741	\leanok
742	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
743	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
744	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
745	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
746	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
747	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
748	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
749	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
750	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
751	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
752	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
753	\end{lemma}
754	
755	\begin{proof}
756	
757	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
758	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
759	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
760	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
761	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
762	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
763	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
764	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
765	\end{proof}

530	/-- **Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial.**
531	
532	If `M, N : X.Modules` are locally trivial of rank one (line bundles), so is
533	their tensor product `tensorObj M N`. Per blueprint
534	`lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`. The proof picks, for each point `x`,
535	a common affine open `W ∋ x` contained in trivialising opens `U` (for `M`) and
536	`U'` (for `N`), refines both trivialisations to `W` via `restrictIsoUnitOfLE`,
537	then transports through `tensorObj_restrict_iso`, the bifunctoriality
538	`tensorObjIsoOfIso`, and the unit isomorphism `tensorObj_unit_iso`:
539	`(M ⊗ N)|_W ≅ M|_W ⊗ N|_W ≅ 𝒪_W ⊗ 𝒪_W ≅ 𝒪_W`. The only residual gap is the
540	substrate-restriction compatibility `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. -/
541	lemma tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
542	    (hM : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial M) (hN : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial N) :
543	    LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial (tensorObj M N) := by
544	  intro x
545	  obtain ⟨U, hxU, hU_aff, ⟨eM⟩⟩ := hM x
546	  obtain ⟨U', hxU', hU'_aff, ⟨eN⟩⟩ := hN x
547	  obtain ⟨W, hW_aff, hxW, hWsub⟩ :=
548	    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := X) (x := x) (U := U ⊓ U') ⟨hxU, hxU'⟩
549	  have hWU : W ≤ U := le_trans hWsub inf_le_left
550	  have hWU' : W ≤ U' := le_trans hWsub inf_le_right
551	  refine ⟨W, hxW, hW_aff, ⟨?_⟩⟩
552	  exact tensorObj_restrict_iso W.ι M N ≪≫
553	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (restrictIsoUnitOfLE hWU eM) (restrictIsoUnitOfLE hWU' eN) ≪≫
554	    tensorObj_unit_iso

556	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — the d.2-free descent re-route (B-connector)
557	
558	The "locally-iso ⇒ iso" half of the descent assembly of `exists_tensorObj_inverse`:
559	a morphism of `𝒪_X`-modules that restricts to an isomorphism on an open
560	neighbourhood of every point is a global isomorphism. The route is the stalkwise
561	iso criterion `TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso` (for sheaves valued
562	in `Ab`, whose forgetful functor reflects isos and preserves limits / filtered
563	colimits) together with `Scheme.Modules.restrictStalkNatIso` (restriction along an
564	open immersion commutes with stalks). **No stalk-⊗ ("d.2") is invoked**: this is a
565	statement about a single module morphism, never about the tensor stalk. -/
566	
567	/-- **B-connector: a morphism of `𝒪_X`-modules that restricts to an isomorphism on
568	an open cover is an isomorphism.** For `φ : M ⟶ N` in `X.Modules`, if every point
569	`x` lies in an open `U x` on which the restriction `(restrictFunctor (U x).ι).map φ`
570	is an isomorphism, then `φ` is an isomorphism. This is the B-bridge of the d.2-free
571	descent re-route assembling `exists_tensorObj_inverse`. -/
572	lemma isIso_of_isIso_restrict {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules} (φ : M ⟶ N)
573	    (U : X → X.Opens) (hxU : ∀ x, x ∈ U x)
574	    (h : ∀ x, IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor (U x).ι).map φ)) :
575	    IsIso φ := by
576	  -- It suffices that each stalk map of the underlying `Ab`-sheaf morphism is iso.
577	  have hst : ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map
578	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ)) := by
579	    intro x
580	    obtain ⟨x', hx'⟩ : ∃ x', (U x).ι x' = x := by
581	      have hmem : x ∈ (U x).ι.opensRange := by
582	        rw [Scheme.Opens.opensRange_ι]; exact hxU x
583	      exact AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.mem_opensRange.mp hmem
584	    haveI : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor (U x).ι).map φ) := h x
585	    -- `(restrictFunctor … ⋙ toPresheaf … ⋙ stalkFunctor x').map φ` is iso (functor of an iso).
586	    haveI hFφ : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor (U x).ι ⋙
587	        Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf _ ⋙ TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x').map φ) := by
588	      dsimp only [Functor.comp_map]; exact Functor.map_isIso _ _
589	    -- Transport the iso across `restrictStalkNatIso` to the stalk at `(U x).ι x' = x`.
590	    have hGφ : IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} ((U x).ι x')).map
591	        ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ)) :=
592	      (CategoryTheory.NatIso.isIso_map_iff
593	        (Scheme.Modules.restrictStalkNatIso (U x).ι x') φ).mp hFφ
594	    exact hx' ▸ hGφ
595	  -- Package as a morphism of `TopCat.Sheaf Ab X` and apply the stalkwise iso criterion.
596	  let MS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩
597	  let NS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨N.presheaf, N.isSheaf⟩
598	  let fS : MS ⟶ NS := ⟨(Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ⟩
599	  haveI : ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map fS.hom) := hst
600	  haveI hSiso : IsIso fS := TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso fS
601	  have h1 : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) := by
602	    have := (TopCat.Sheaf.forget Ab.{u} X).map_isIso fS
603	    exact this
604	  exact (CategoryTheory.isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso φ (Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X)).mp h1
605	
606	/-- **A-bridge step (ii): promote an `𝒪_X`-linear `Ab`-presheaf morphism to a module
607	morphism.** Given a morphism `g : M.presheaf ⟶ N.presheaf` of the underlying
608	`Ab`-presheaves that is sectionwise `𝒪_X`-linear, package it as a morphism `M ⟶ N`
609	of `𝒪_X`-modules. This wraps `PresheafOfModules.homMk` at the `Scheme.Modules` level;
610	it is the "promote to `𝒪_X`-linear" half of the descent A-bridge `homOfLocalCompat`
611	(the ab-sheaf gluing produces the linear `g`; this lemma turns it into a module map).
612	Sectionwise linearity is a property the consumer checks on a separated presheaf. -/
613	noncomputable def homMk {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
614	    (g : M.val.presheaf ⟶ N.val.presheaf)
615	    (hg : ∀ (V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ) (r : X.ringCatSheaf.obj.obj V) (m : M.val.obj V),
616	      (g.app V).hom (r • m) = r • (g.app V).hom m) :
617	    M ⟶ N :=
618	  ⟨PresheafOfModules.homMk (M₁ := M.val) (M₂ := N.val) g hg⟩
